Row1.Pathway ID/Name: RET signaling / RTK axis (KEGG hsa04010 overlap)
Row1.Rationale: RET fusions constitutively activate RET kinase via docking tyrosines such as Y1062 and Y1096, driving multiple oncogenic cascades. Pralsetinib directly inhibits RET autophosphorylation, suppressing downstream signaling and producing clinical responses in RET fusion–positive tumors.

Row2.Rationale: RET fusion proteins activate the MAPK cascade to promote proliferation and survival. Inhibition of RET by pralsetinib suppresses MAPK signaling in sensitive tumors, reducing mitogenic signaling and tumor growth.

Row3.Rationale: RET Y1062 recruits adaptor proteins that activate PI3K/AKT signaling to support survival and growth. RET inhibition by pralsetinib indirectly suppresses this pathway, contributing to tumor regression.

Row4.Rationale: RET phosphorylates docking sites such as Y752 and Y928 that activate STAT3 and JAK–STAT signaling, promoting proliferation and immune modulation. RET inhibition by pralsetinib suppresses this signaling axis in responsive tumors.

Row5.Pathway ID/Name: PKC / PLCγ and PKA-related signaling (KEGG hsa04010 component)
Row5.Rationale: RET Y1015 recruits PLCγ leading to PKC activation, while other docking sites can engage PKA signaling pathways that enhance migration and survival. Pralsetinib suppresses these RET-dependent signaling inputs.

Row6.Pathway ID/Name: Cell cycle regulation (CDK–RB–E2F modules; KEGG hsa04110)
Row6.Rationale: RET-driven MAPK and PI3K signaling promotes cyclin/CDK activation and RB–E2F mediated G1–S transition. Inhibition of RET by pralsetinib reduces mitogenic signaling, enforcing cell-cycle arrest in RET fusion tumors.
